metrics.title: Academic Metrics
metrics.subtitle: Indicators for textual transmission analysis
metrics.stabilizationDescription: The New Testament reached 90% coverage by century {century}

history.title: Historical Context
history.subtitle: Transmission of the New Testament text
history.papyriOriginText: The New Testament papyri come mainly from Egypt, where the dry climate preserved organic materials for centuries. Notable discoveries include those from Oxyrhynchus (1897–1907), which revealed thousands of fragments; the Chester Beatty Papyri (1930s), with nearly complete codices of Paul and the Gospels; and the Bodmer Papyri (1950s), including P66 and P75 with Johannine and Gospel texts of great antiquity.
history.codexDevelopmentText: The transition from the scroll (volumen) to the codex (book with pages) was decisive for the transmission of the text. The codex allowed multiple books to be gathered in a single volume, facilitated consultation and comparison of passages, and became the standard format for Christian writings as early as the 2nd century. P52 (c. 125–175), the oldest NT fragment, is already in codex format.
history.greatCodexText: The 4th century produced the great uncial manuscripts containing the complete or nearly complete NT: the Codex Sinaiticus (א, 01), discovered at St. Catherine's Monastery; the Codex Vaticanus (B, 03), preserved in the Vatican Apostolic Library; and the Codex Alexandrinus (A, 02), of Alexandrian provenance. These witnesses are fundamental for textual criticism and the reconstruction of the original text.
history.textualExpansion: Textual Expansion up to the 10th Century
history.textualExpansionText: From the 5th century onward, minuscule (cursive) script gradually replaced uncial in manuscripts. Scriptoria in Byzantine monasteries, such as those in Constantinople and Mount Athos, copied and preserved the text on a large scale. The majority of the more than 5,800 Greek NT manuscripts are minuscules from the 9th–16th centuries, witnessing the expansion and stabilization of the Byzantine textual tradition.

manuscriptCount.title: How Many Manuscripts Exist?
manuscriptCount.subtitle: Total Greek New Testament manuscripts
manuscriptCount.gregoryAlandDescription: The Gregory-Aland cataloging system is the international standard for identifying Greek New Testament manuscripts. Created by Caspar René Gregory and updated by Kurt Aland, it classifies manuscripts into four categories:
manuscriptCount.papyriDescription: Written on papyrus, these are the oldest witnesses. Designated with the prefix P followed by a number (e.g.: P52, P66, P75). Most come from Egypt, dating from the 2nd–7th centuries.
manuscriptCount.uncialsDescription: Parchment manuscripts written in uppercase (uncial) letters. They include the great codices such as Sinaiticus (01), Vaticanus (03), and Alexandrinus (02). Dating from the 3rd–10th centuries.
manuscriptCount.minusculesDescription: Manuscripts in cursive minuscule script, predominant from the 9th century onward. They represent the majority of NT manuscripts, numbered from 1 to 2900+.
manuscriptCount.lectionariesDescription: Liturgical manuscripts containing NT passages organized for public reading in worship. Designated with the prefix l (e.g.: l1, l2).

sources.title: Data Sources
sources.subtitle: Origin, methodology, and data limitations
sources.primarySourceText1: The manuscriptological data is obtained from the {ntvmr}, maintained by the Institute for New Testament Textual Research (INTF) at the University of Münster, Germany.
sources.primarySourceText2: The NTVMR API exposes transcriptions in TEI/XML format. For each manuscript, the system queries the transcription endpoint with the parameter {formatParam}, which returns XML markup with {abElement} elements indicating which verses are present in the manuscript.
sources.primarySourceText3: The ingestion flow parses the XML, extracts the present verses, and links each one to the manuscript record in the database. When the NTVMR API is unavailable or does not return data for a specific manuscript, the system uses ranges defined in seed data as a fallback.
sources.gregoryAlandText1: The Gregory-Aland cataloging system assigns unique identifiers to each Greek New Testament manuscript. The numbering follows conventions by type:
sources.gregoryAlandUncials: Uncials: numbering 01–0323+ (or Greek/Latin letters for the oldest)
sources.gregoryAlandText2: The catalog is maintained by the INTF and updated as new manuscripts are discovered or reclassified.
sources.datingMethodologyText1: For manuscripts with a date range (e.g., "2nd/3rd century"), the system adopts a {conservative} approach: it uses the earliest century of the range ({centuryMin}). This means that if a manuscript is dated between the 2nd and 3rd centuries, it is counted as a 2nd-century attestation.
sources.datingMethodologyText2: The {cumulativeCoverage} for a given century N includes all manuscripts from centuries 1 to N. Thus, 5th-century coverage includes 2nd-century papyri, 3rd-century uncials, and so on. A verse appearing in multiple manuscripts is counted only once (deduplication).
sources.limitationFragmentary: Many papyri and uncials are fragments that preserve only portions of books. The system accurately records which verses are present, but smaller fragments contribute less to total coverage.
sources.limitationDating: The dating of ancient manuscripts is based on paleographic analysis (writing style), which has a margin of error of 50–100 years. The dates used are consensus scholarly estimates, not absolute dates.
sources.limitationVariants: This system measures {coverage} (whether a verse exists in at least one manuscript), not {textualVariation} (differences between manuscripts). The existence of a verse in a manuscript does not imply that its text is identical to that of other manuscripts.
sources.councilSourcesIntro: Council data (year, location, participants, canons) is assembled from multiple historical sources. Each source is weighted by reliability (primary, academic, structured, aggregator). The confidence score reflects agreement across these sources.
sources.curatedSeedDescription: Project-curated list based on multiple documentary and academic references (Schaff, Hefele, Catholic Encyclopedia, etc.). Used as the foundation for council records and to fill gaps when external sources are unavailable.
sources.councilSourcesOutro: Other council sources include Fordham Medieval Sourcebook, Philip Schaff, Karl Hefele, Catholic Encyclopedia, Wikidata, and Wikipedia. See the Sources tab on each council detail page for the full list.

faq.title: Academic FAQ
faq.subtitle: Frequently asked questions about NT manuscriptology
faq.q1: Was the New Testament altered over the centuries?
faq.a1: The manuscript tradition of the NT is extraordinarily rich. With more than 5,800 Greek manuscripts, it is possible to reconstruct the original text with a high degree of confidence. Most textual variants are trivial copying errors (spelling, word order) that do not affect meaning. Significant variants represent less than 1% of the text, and no central Christian doctrine depends on disputed passages.
faq.q2: How many verses do not appear in the oldest manuscripts?
faq.a2_part1: In manuscripts up to the 3rd century (papyri), NT coverage already exceeds 40% of verses. By the 5th century, with the great uncial codices, coverage surpasses 90%. Verses "absent" from the oldest manuscripts does not mean they did not exist — only that the manuscripts containing them did not survive. Check the
faq.a2_part2: to visualize coverage evolution by century.
faq.q3: Are there NT books that only appear in the 4th century?
faq.a3: There are no NT books that only appear from the 4th century onward. All 27 books have earlier manuscript attestation. However, shorter books like 2 John, 3 John, and Jude have fewer ancient witnesses simply because they are short (1 chapter) — there are fewer opportunities for preservation. The Gospels and Pauline epistles have solid papyrological attestation since the 2nd century.
faq.q4: What is the Pericope of the Adulteress?
faq.a4: The passage of John 7:53–8:11 (Pericope Adulterae) does not appear in the oldest manuscripts, such as P66, P75, Sinaiticus, and Vaticanus. It is considered by most scholars to be a later addition to the Gospel of John, although it may preserve an authentic tradition. This system records the exact coverage of verses, allowing you to verify in which manuscripts the passage appears.
faq.q5: What is the oldest New Testament manuscript?
faq.a5_part1: P52 (Rylands Papyrus 457) is generally considered the oldest NT manuscript, dated between 125–175 AD. It contains fragments of John 18:31–33 and 18:37–38. Other early papyri include P90 (John, 2nd c.), P104 (Matthew, 2nd c.), and the Chester Beatty Papyri set (P45, P46, P47) from the 3rd century. Explore the details on the
faq.apologistQuestions: Common questions from a Christian apologist
faq.q6: How many manuscripts do we have compared to other ancient works?
faq.a6: The NT has more than 5,800 Greek manuscripts, plus ~10,000 Latin manuscripts and thousands in other languages (Syriac, Coptic, etc.). For comparison: Homer's Iliad has ~1,800 manuscripts; Plato's works, ~250; Tacitus's Annals, ~20 copies. No work from Antiquity comes close to the NT in volume of manuscript evidence.
faq.q7: Is the NT the best-attested ancient text?
faq.a7: Yes, both in number of manuscripts and in temporal proximity to the original. The interval between the composition of the NT books (c. 50–100 AD) and the earliest manuscripts (c. 125–200 AD) is only 25–150 years. For most classical works, this interval is 800–1400 years.
faq.q8: What is the century of textual stabilization?
faq.a8_part1: The concept of "stabilization" indicates the century in which cumulative coverage reaches 90% of verses. For the NT as a whole, this generally occurs between the 4th and 5th centuries. Check the
faq.a8_part2: to see the stabilization century for each individual book.
faq.q9: What is the coverage percentage up to the 3rd century?
faq.a9_part1: Coverage varies by book. The Gospels of John and Matthew have significant coverage thanks to papyri like P66, P75, P45. Pauline epistles are also well attested via P46. Shorter books like 2 Peter and Jude have less attestation in this period. Use the
faq.a9_part2: with the century slider to explore the data in real time.
faq.a10_part1: The Fragmentation Index measures how fragmentary the attestation of a book is. Calculated as {formula}. Values close to 0 indicate few manuscripts per verse (high fragmentation); values close to 1 indicate many manuscripts attesting each verse (low fragmentation). Available on the
faq.a11: The growth rate of coverage between consecutive centuries. Calculated as {formula}. A high growth rate from the 3rd to 4th century reflects the contribution of the great uncial codices. Decreasing rates indicate that coverage is stabilizing.
faq.a12: The ratio of covered verses to number of manuscripts: {formula}. High values indicate that each manuscript contributes many unique verses. Low values indicate redundancy (many manuscripts covering the same verses).
faq.a13_part1: Cumulative coverage for century N includes all manuscripts from centuries 1 to N. A verse is "covered" if it appears in at least one manuscript up to the century considered. Each verse is counted only once, regardless of how many manuscripts contain it. This means that coverage is monotonically increasing — it never decreases from one century to the next. Visualize on the

bibleIngestion.title: Bible
bibleIngestion.confirmRunLayer: Run all phases of this layer?
bibleIngestion.confirmClearGlosses: This will delete ALL translated glosses (PT/ES) and ALL word alignments (KJV/ARC69). You will need to re-run translation and alignment phases. Continue?
bibleIngestion.layer1Subtitle: Version, book and abbreviation seeds (run once). Prerequisite for all layers.
bibleIngestion.layer1Description: Creates the Bible system foundation: 4 versions (KJV, AA, ACF, ARC69), 66 books with names in 3 languages, 31,102 verses, and abbreviations for the reference parser. Run once — prerequisite for everything.
bibleIngestion.layer2Subtitle: Interlinear (TAGNT), lexicon and AI translations. Requires Layer 1.
bibleIngestion.layer2Description: Imports Greek interlinear (TAGNT) and Hebrew with 5,400+ Greek lexicon entries (Strong's) and 8,600+ Hebrew. Enriches with BibleHub data (definitions, origins, exhaustive uses). Translates glosses, lexicons and enrichments to pt/es via LLM Queue (Haiku for glosses, Sonnet for lexicons).
bibleIngestion.layer3Subtitle: Verse-by-verse texts per version. Independent of Layer 2.
bibleIngestion.layer3Description: Imports verse-by-verse text for each Bible version. KJV and AA come from GitHub JSON; ACF and ARC69 via web scraper (bibliaonline.com.br). Independent of Layer 2 — can run in parallel.
bibleIngestion.layer4Subtitle: Word-to-word alignment via AI. Requires Layers 2 + 3.
bibleIngestion.layer4Description: Aligns word-by-word between Greek/Hebrew interlinear and KJV/ARC69 texts using AI (Opus via LLM Queue). Produces the mapping that lets users click on a word to see the corresponding Greek/Hebrew original. Only KJV (en) and ARC69 (pt) receive alignment.

phaseDescriptions.bible_seed_versions: Loads metadata for 4 Bible versions (KJV, AA, ACF, ARC69). Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_seed_abbreviations: Book abbreviations (pt/en/es) for reference parser. Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_ingest_nt_interlinear: Imports TAGNT: Greek words, transliteration, glosses, Strong's and morphology. Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_ingest_ot_interlinear: Imports Hebrew interlinear (OSHB) from OT. Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_ingest_greek_lexicon: Downloads complete Greek lexicon (STEPBible TBESG): Strong's, lemmas, definitions. Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_ingest_hebrew_lexicon: Downloads complete Hebrew lexicon (STEPBible TBESH): Strong's, lemmas, definitions. Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_fill_missing_hebrew: Fills missing Hebrew entries via BibleHub scraper. Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_translate_lexicon: Translates Greek lexicon definitions to pt/es via LLM Queue (Sonnet). Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_translate_hebrew_lexicon: Translates Hebrew lexicon definitions to pt/es via LLM Queue (Sonnet). Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_translate_glosses: Translates interlinear glosses to pt/es via LLM Queue (Haiku). Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_enrich_greek_lexicon: Enriches Greek lexicon with KJV translations, origins and phonetics via BibleHub. Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_enrich_hebrew_lexicon: Enriches Hebrew lexicon with KJV translations, origins and phonetics via BibleHub. Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_reenrich_greek_lexicon: Re-enriches incomplete Greek entries. On demand.
phaseDescriptions.bible_reenrich_hebrew_lexicon: Re-enriches incomplete Hebrew entries. On demand.
phaseDescriptions.bible_translate_enrichment_greek: Translates enriched Greek fields (KJV, origins, NAS) to pt/es via LLM Queue (Haiku). Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_translate_enrichment_hebrew: Translates enriched Hebrew fields to pt/es via LLM Queue (Haiku). Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_ingest_text_kjv: Imports KJV text (English) from GitHub JSON. Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_ingest_text_aa: Imports Almeida Atualizada text from GitHub JSON. Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_ingest_text_acf: Imports Almeida Corrigida Fiel text via scraper. Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_ingest_text_arc69: Imports ARC69 text via scraper (bibliaonline.com.br). Run once.
phaseDescriptions.bible_align_kjv: Aligns Greek → KJV word-by-word via AI (Opus). Filtered by bible-ingestion-filter.txt.
phaseDescriptions.bible_align_arc69: Aligns Greek → ARC69 word-by-word via AI (Opus). Filtered by bible-ingestion-filter.txt.
phaseDescriptions.bible_align_hebrew_kjv: Aligns Hebrew → KJV word-by-word via AI (Opus). Filtered by bible-ingestion-filter.txt.
phaseDescriptions.bible_align_hebrew_arc69: Aligns Hebrew → ARC69 word-by-word via AI (Opus). Filtered by bible-ingestion-filter.txt.

councils.confidenceExplainer: Agreement score across historical sources consulted. The more sources agree on year, location, and participants, the higher the confidence.
councils.confidenceHigh: High confidence — most sources agree on the key facts.
councils.confidenceMedium: Moderate confidence — some divergence between sources.
councils.confidenceLow: Low confidence — significant disagreement or few sources available.
councils.methodologyText: Each source is weighted by reliability (primary sources = 1.0, academic = 0.8, structured = 0.7, aggregators = 0.5). The score reflects weighted agreement across year, location, and participant data.

methodology.title: Dating Methodology
methodology.subtitle: How the system determines and classifies manuscript and patristic dating
methodology.overviewText1: The system stores dating at two precision levels: **centuries** (century_min / century_max) and **years** (year_min / year_max / year_best).
methodology.overviewText2: Centuries are used for cumulative coverage calculations — they determine which manuscripts contribute to each century's coverage statistics. Years enable precise temporal analysis and timeline visualization.
methodology.overviewText3: Not all records have year-level dating. When unavailable, the system falls back to century-level data, which is always present for cataloged manuscripts.
methodology.manuscriptSourcesText1: The primary source for manuscript dating is the **NTVMR** (New Testament Virtual Manuscript Room), maintained by the Institut für Neutestamentliche Textforschung at the University of Münster.
methodology.manuscriptSourcesText2: The NTVMR API provides {origEarly} and {origLate} fields for each manuscript — these represent real academic cataloging years based on paleographic analysis and scholarly consensus.
methodology.manuscriptSourcesText3: These values are preserved as {yearMin} / {yearMax} with {confidenceHigh}. NTVMR is the world's primary academic database for Greek New Testament manuscript cataloging.
methodology.manuscriptSourcesText4: NTVMR data is never overwritten by AI-generated estimates.
methodology.patristicSourcesText1: Unlike manuscripts, there is no single public API with standardized dating for all Church Fathers. Academic databases exist but lack uniform machine-readable date fields.
methodology.patristicSourcesText2: To fill this gap, the system uses the **OpenAI API** (gpt-4o-mini model) to obtain dating estimates. A structured prompt requests: birth year, death year, floruit period, and academic reference for the dating.
methodology.patristicSourcesText3: All AI-generated dating receives {confidenceLow} until manually reviewed and validated by an administrator.
methodology.patristicSourcesText4: This process is **triggered manually by administrators** — it is never automatic or scheduled.
methodology.confidenceIntro: Every year-level dating record carries a confidence indicator that reflects the reliability of its source:
methodology.confidenceHighDesc: Paleographic analysis and scholarly consensus from established institutions
methodology.confidenceMediumDesc: Hand-curated data from reliable secondary references, reviewed before insertion
methodology.confidenceLowDesc: LLM-generated estimates awaiting manual validation by administrators
methodology.confidenceRule: Confidence levels are **never auto-promoted**. LOW data cannot become MEDIUM or HIGH through any automated process — only manual administrator review can elevate a record's confidence.
methodology.yearBestText1: The {yearBest} field represents the scholarly consensus year for a given record. For example, Irenaeus has {example} (floruit circa 180 AD).
methodology.yearBestText2: This field is only filled when the source **explicitly provides a specific accepted year**. It is **never calculated as a mathematical average** of yearMin and yearMax.
methodology.yearBestText3: When no scholarly consensus exists for a specific year, the field remains empty (null). This avoids presenting misleading precision for records where only a date range is known.
methodology.enrichmentIntro: The dating enrichment process is a manual, administrator-triggered operation that fills in missing year-level dating for Church Fathers:
methodology.enrichmentStep3: For each record, sends a structured prompt to OpenAI requesting birth year, death year, floruit, and academic reference
methodology.enrichmentStep6: Every result is logged for audit, including both successful enrichments and failures

wikiLlm.title: LLM Architecture Wiki
wikiLlm.subtitle: How the multi-provider LLM orchestration works with priority-based fallback
wikiLlm.overviewText1: The system uses an **LLM Orchestrator** to route all AI requests (summarization, translation, dating enrichment, conflict resolution) through a unified interface that supports multiple LLM providers.
wikiLlm.overviewText2: Instead of being locked into a single provider, the orchestrator tries providers in a configured priority order. If the primary provider fails (API error, timeout, rate limit, billing issue), it automatically falls back to the next available provider.
wikiLlm.overviewText3: This architecture provides **high availability**, cost optimization, and makes it trivial to add new providers in the future without modifying any business logic.
wikiLlm.providerIntro: The following providers are currently configured, attempted in order of priority:
wikiLlm.flowIntro: When a service (e.g., BiographySummarizationService) needs to call an LLM, the following steps occur:
wikiLlm.flowStep1: The service builds an LlmRequest with systemPrompt, userContent, temperature, and a label for logging.
wikiLlm.flowStep3: The orchestrator filters providers that have an API key configured (isAvailable = true).
wikiLlm.flowStep4: It attempts the highest-priority available provider first (Anthropic).
wikiLlm.flowStep5: If the call succeeds, it returns an LlmResponse with the content, provider name, and model used.
wikiLlm.flowStep6: If it fails, the error is logged and the next provider is attempted. If all fail, an exception is thrown.
wikiLlm.diagramIntro: Visual representation of the request flow through the orchestration layer:
wikiLlm.scenarioAStep1Detail: Service sends LlmRequest to the orchestrator
wikiLlm.scenarioAStep2Detail: Rate limiter enforces pacing, request sent to Claude Opus 4.6
wikiLlm.scenarioAStep3Detail: Response returned directly to the service. OpenAI is never called.
wikiLlm.scenarioBTitle: Scenario B — Fallback to Secondary Provider
wikiLlm.scenarioBStep1Detail: Service sends LlmRequest to the orchestrator
wikiLlm.scenarioBStep2Detail: 429 rate limit after 5 retries, or timeout, or API error
wikiLlm.scenarioBStep3Detail: Error logged, orchestrator moves to OpenAI provider
wikiLlm.scenarioBStep4Detail: Response returned to the service from fallback provider
wikiLlm.errorText1: If **all providers fail**, the orchestrator throws LlmAllProvidersFailedException containing the error from each provider. The calling service handles this gracefully.
wikiLlm.errorText2: Each provider has its own rate limiter with exponential backoff + jitter to avoid thundering herd problems. Non-retryable errors (billing, authentication) skip retries immediately.
wikiLlm.errorNonRetryableText: Billing issues (billing_not_active), invalid API keys, and account deactivation are detected immediately and skip to the next provider without wasting retry attempts.
wikiLlm.errorGraceful1: Biography summarization: returns original text without summarizing
wikiLlm.errorGraceful3: Dating enrichment: skips the record, continues with next
wikiLlm.errorGraceful4: Academic text extraction: falls back to heuristic regex extraction
wikiLlm.extensibilityText1: The architecture follows the **Open/Closed Principle** — adding a new provider requires zero changes to existing services or the orchestrator logic.
wikiLlm.extensibilityStep1: Create a new class implementing LlmProvider interface
wikiLlm.extensibilityStep2: Add the new enum value to LlmProviderType
wikiLlm.extensibilityStep3: Register the provider in Application.kt and add it to the orchestrator's provider list
wikiLlm.extensibilityStep4: Configure the API key via environment variable — done
wikiLlm.extensibilityDbText: Provider priority is currently hardcoded in LlmConfig. A future enhancement will store priority configuration in a database table, allowing runtime changes without redeployment.
